I ran across this self-proclaimed copper bull on my feed, and the guy was literally wishing a death curse on the entire tech complex—EVs, AI, the whole nine yards.

Honestly, I was completely dumbfounded. I’ve been sitting on a massive book of copper equities for years now, but if you’re long Dr. Copper, you should be aggressively praying that Jensen Huang and Sam Altman don't get locked up and AI data centers don't spontaneously combust.

Legacy copper demand has been flatlining for a minute now. We've all heard the "China property sector is toast" macro tape until we're blue in the face. Granted this cycle is heavily supply-driven, but the demand side is anchored to the high tech narrative.

It's the energy transition and data centers that are holding up the structural demand thesis. This frontier demand is ramping on a hockey-stick trajectory; EVs were the headline act early on, but grid and transmission capex are rapidly becoming the real heavy lifters.

Backstopped by the AI revolution and this insatiable hunger for power, global grid capex is compounding exponentially, and copper is running the textbook picks and shovels play.

Also structurally speaking, an EV gobbles up almost 3x the copper intensity of a legacy ICE vehicle, meaning EVs already occupy a massive chunk of the demand pie chart.

To top it off AI data centers are more than double the copper-intensity of a vanilla crypto mining warehouse. The data center demand vector is moving in a straight line up and right.

And yet, some guys still want Tesla and Nvidia to go to zero? Give me a break. We need these tech nerds to keep printing.

Earlier today, I gave this comment on #gold to @business: 
Considering what was thrown at gold yesterday - surging oil and fuel prices, hawkish signals from the FOMC minutes, rising yields and traders once again entertaining the possibility of a rate hike - it has actually held up remarkably well. The metal found support ahead of USD 4,050 and is now bouncing as the dollar and yields trade softer.
In fact, the dollar has traded broadly unchanged over the past two days, potentially signalling some buying fatigue among traders already holding a very elevated speculative long position. Overall, the price action over the past 24 hours strengthens my belief that gold has moved from capitulation to consolidation.
That view could obviously be challenged if oil prices continue to rise. However, given the recent weakness in US jobs data, I do not think the FOMC will automatically reach for the rate-hike button, even if higher energy prices increase the risk of inflation remaining elevated for longer.

Fed Chairman Warsh forms team of external experts to review monetary policy framework Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh formed five working groups to conduct a comprehensive review of the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy operating mechanism, covering areas such as balance sheet management, policy tools, and the impact of artificial intelligence.

The Fed stated that each working group will operate independently, conduct research based on facts, and submit rigorous analysis results to the Federal Open Market Committee. The working group will assess whether there is room for improvement in policy tools, analytical methods and policy frameworks.

The review team includes many well-known economists and former central bank officials.

Among them, Harvard economist Raj Chetty will co-lead the data working group, technology investor Marc Andreessen will be in charge of the productivity and employment working group, and former White House Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Greg Mankiw will co-lead the inflation working group. Warsh said that the U.S.

economy has undergone tremendous changes in the past generation, and the current transformation is faster. The Federal Reserve needs to ensure that it is in the best condition to achieve the dual goals of stabilizing prices and promoting employment.

Federal Reserve announces list of leaders of "Five Major Reform Working Groups" The Federal Reserve announced the leadership and goals of the working group. Federal Reserve Chairman Warsh said that the goal of the working group is to put the Fed in the best position to achieve its goals.

Each ad hoc working group will operate independently and be supported by Fed staff; the working group will be committed to improving methods and tools; its commitment to price stability and maximum employment is "unwavering." The five working groups are: Inflation Working Group, Balance Sheet Working Group, Employment Working Group, Data Working Group, and Communication Working Group.

The leaders of the working group include a number of former global central bank governors, including King, Rajan, Fraga and former Federal Reserve Board Governor Stan.

The United States decides not to impose tariffs on aircraft and jet parts for the time being After completing relevant investigations, the United States decided not to impose tariffs on commercial aircraft, jet engines and their parts for the time being.

US President Trump instead directed the government to negotiate with trading partners to adjust the import of such products. Previously, a report submitted by U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick determined that imported commercial aircraft, jet engines and related parts pose a threat to U.S. national security.

The investigation’s findings leave room for future tariffs, which Trump could impose if the U.S. fails to reach a deal with trading partners to resolve the issue.

According to the announcement document released by the White House, if the relevant agreement cannot be reached within 180 days, or the agreement is "ineffective," Trump "may also take other actions deemed necessary" to eliminate this threat.

OpenAI officially launched the GPT-5.6 series of models; after consultation with the Trump administration, it made many adjustments to GPT-5.6 Following the previous limited preview, OpenAI officially launched the GPT-5.6 series of models, which are open to the public. The GPT-5.6 model is charged per million Tokens and includes 3 model specifications: the Sol model input is US$5 and the output is US$30; the Terra model input is US$2.50 and the output is US$15; the Luna model input is US$1 and the output is US$6.

GPT-5.6 is set to be made available to the public on Thursday after OpenAI said it received approval from U.S. government leadership. Altman did not elaborate on the specific adjustments OpenAI has made along the way, but said the government is testing new models and looking for potential issues.

ING: Le Pen returns to the 2027 French presidential election and financial risks remain a core variable in the market ING said in its latest report that Marine Le Pen, leader of the French far-right National Rally (RN), has regained the qualification to run in the 2027 presidential election, but its legal uncertainty, French political fragmentation and fiscal deterioration prospects will jointly increase the risk of election war and market volatility.

The report pointed out that Le Pen was found guilty in March 2025 of the European Parliament Assistant Fund Abuse Case. She was originally sentenced to 4 years in prison, with 2 years of probation, and was banned from holding public office for 5 years.

The Court of Appeal upheld the conviction, but adjusted the sentence to 3 years, 2 of which were suspended, and the other 1 year to be executed by electronic monitoring; the public office ban was adjusted to 45 months, of which 30 months were suspended.

Since the first 15 months of the ban have expired from March 2025, Le Pen can therefore participate in the 2027 presidential election. Le Pen has announced that she will run on behalf of the RN and said that if she wins, Jordan Bardera will be the prime minister.

However, she has decided to appeal to the Supreme Court, which is expected to rule in early 2027, and legal risks may still hang over the campaign. In terms of polls, the RN candidate's current support rate is about 35%, clearly ahead of the 15% to 20% of centrist candidates, and the 10% to 15% of left-wing candidates such as Mélenchon and Glucksmann.

ING believes that RN will most likely lead in the first round, but its opponents in the second round are still highly uncertain, depending on whether the left wing is integrated and whether the center is dominated by Edouard Philippe or Gabriel Attal.

At the policy level, RN has downplayed its proposals for "leaving the Eurozone" and a referendum on EU membership, and the market is less concerned about its potential victory than before.

However, the party still criticizes EU fiscal rules, advocates reducing production taxes and value-added taxes, has an unclear position on pensions, and lacks a clear plan on how to stabilize public debt in the long term. France's financial pressure will become the main theme of the campaign.

The government has lowered its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 0.7% from the budget assumption of 1.0%, and the target of reducing the deficit rate to 5.0% this year from 5.1% in 2025 is more difficult to achieve.

The European Commission estimates that if the policy remains unchanged, France's deficit rate will rise to 5.7% in 2027, and the public debt rate will reach 120.2%.

ING said that the bond market is more concerned about fiscal improvement than the candidates themselves, and the spread of 10-year French bonds relative to German bonds has returned to around 80 basis points; in the foreign exchange market, the euro against the Swiss franc will be the clearest indicator of political risk in France.

Goldman Sachs: The Fed's baseline scenario for the year is still to hold on to the inflation path and decide whether to raise or cut interest rates.

Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the minutes of the Federal Reserve’s June meeting showed that all FOMC participants supported maintaining the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, but a few participants believed that there was reason to raise interest rates at that time.

The minutes showed that the Fed's policy path is still highly dependent on inflation trends, rather than a unidirectional shift to easing or tightening. The research report stated that most participants discussed different macro scenarios.

If inflationary pressures subside soon and begin to return to near the 2% target, almost all relevant participants believe that keeping interest rates unchanged or eventually cutting interest rates may be appropriate options.

On the contrary, if inflation continues to be high due to AI-related demand, conflict in the Middle East, or the impact of tariffs, almost all relevant participants believe that further tightening of policy may be necessary. The minutes of the meeting showed that participants generally believed that U.S.

inflation has risen further and is significantly higher than the 2% target, due to tariffs, supply chain disruptions, and AI investment demand.

"Several" participants pointed out that price pressures have become broader, involving areas such as transportation, air tickets, petrochemicals and agricultural inputs; service sector inflation remains high after excluding housing.

Most participants also believed that AI-related investment demand pushes the economy to grow faster than potential, which may make inflation more sustainable. However, the labor market was seen as remaining stable, with many participants saying it was not currently a source of inflationary pressures.

Economic activity is still expanding at a steady pace, mainly supported by AI-related business investment; higher stock prices and previous tax rebates have also boosted consumption, especially spending by high-income households. Goldman Sachs predicts that the U.S.

core PCE growth rate will drop from the current 3.4% to 3.0% in December 2026, and the core CPI will drop from the current 2.9% to 2.6%. The core CPI month-on-month readings in the coming months are expected to be moderate.

The bank believes that although there are still risks of interest rate hikes this year, the baseline scenario is still for the Federal Reserve to maintain policy interest rates unchanged in 2026.

Goldman Sachs: Tensions in Hormuz may hinder the recovery of Middle East oil supplies Goldman Sachs estimated in a July 8 research report that crude oil production in the Persian Gulf region in June was still about 10.5 million barrels per day lower than pre-war levels.

Analysts at the bank pointed out that although Middle Eastern oil-producing countries have begun to restart previously shut down oil wells in the past month, if the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted again, it may slow down the recovery of production.

Goldman Sachs said recent attacks on oil tankers showed that the risk of crossing the Strait of Hormuz remains high. With the status of the ceasefire unclear, shipping companies may delay passage, suppressing short-term oil flows.

The bank estimates that Persian Gulf oil transportation volume has fallen back to about 70% of normal levels after the recent attacks on oil tankers; previously, in the first 10 days after the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, transportation volume once returned to more than 80% of pre-war levels.

Goldman Sachs believes that Persian Gulf oil flows and oil prices currently face two-way risks.

If negotiations continue in the next 60 days, shipping safety is guaranteed, and Iranian crude oil sales exemptions are renewed, transportation volume is expected to resume before the end of July; if negotiations fail and tanker attacks escalate, traffic may decline further.

Previously, with the resumption of navigation to Hormuz, Goldman Sachs had lowered its oil price forecast and warned that there is still a risk of oversupply reappearing in the crude oil market.

Goldman Sachs: Food and energy security policies may increase agricultural product price volatility Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that global agricultural supply is highly concentrated, with the top three exporters of key varieties such as corn, soybeans, rice, sugar and palm oil accounting for 60% to 90% of global trade volume, making the agricultural product market more vulnerable to local weather, geopolitical and policy shocks.

The bank believes that what really amplifies price fluctuations is not just the decline in production, but the protectionist measures taken by exporting countries to ensure domestic food and energy security.

Once there is a supply disturbance, or even just a disturbance in expectations, policies such as export restrictions, mandatory increases in biofuel blending ratios, and inventory hoarding may reduce exportable supply, reduce liquidity in the global trade market, and amplify price reactions.

Goldman Sachs mentioned that India accounts for about 40% of global rice exports. It banned the export of non-Basmati rice in 2023 due to concerns about El Niño. Although India's output ultimately remained resilient that year, rice prices in Asia rose significantly as a result.

Similarly, India recently restricted sugar exports due to the risk of a potential "super" El Niño. Energy security could also squeeze food supplies. From 2016 to 2026, Indonesia increased the blending ratio of biodiesel from 20% to 40% to 50%.

However, its palm oil production has limited growth due to land and yield constraints, resulting in a decline in exportable palm oil balances and pushing up the bottom of vegetable oil prices.

Goldman Sachs believes that agricultural product prices face three major upward risks in the future: El Niño has emerged and has a 63% probability of developing into a "super" El Niño; higher energy prices in the first half of 2026 may prompt more countries to increase their biofuel requirements; and if the Strait of Hormuz is disturbed again, it will affect the nitrogen fertilizer purchasing season in the third quarter.

The bank said that although protectionist policies are intended to enhance domestic resilience, they may exacerbate fragmentation and price volatility in global agricultural markets. In terms of Alphabet, Goldman Sachs emphasized that it is still one of the most leveraged companies in the AI theme and raised its Google Cloud revenue forecast.

It is expected to grow by 71% year-on-year in 2026 and 65% in 2027, reaching approximately US$400 billion by 2030, with a five-year compound growth rate of approximately 47%. However, demand for AI computing power continues to exceed supply, which has also pushed up capital expenditures.

Goldman Sachs estimates that Alphabet’s cumulative capital expenditures from 2026 to 2028 will be approximately US$790 billion; Meta’s cumulative capital expenditures during the same period will be approximately US$534 billion.

Goldman Sachs said that Meta’s exploration of monetizing idle computing power in the future does not mean that it is slowing down its investment in AI, but is more likely to take advantage of the mismatch between supply and demand in a phased manner.

The bank believes that the return on AI capital expenditure is still the core controversy that investors are most concerned about.

Goldman Sachs: Nearline HDD demand continues to be strong Goldman Sachs said in its latest research report that after holding an expert conference call with Techno Systems Research, it once again confirmed that the nearline HDD supply and demand pattern is still healthy and tight, and that demand for AI data centers and cloud storage will continue to support the prosperity of the HDD industry chain.

The bank maintains a "buy" rating on TDK and HOYA, and is optimistic about the benefit space of TDK in the field of HDD heads and suspensions and HOYA in the field of glass substrates respectively.

TSR predicts that the global HDD total addressable market (TAM) in 2026 will be 129.1 million units, a year-on-year increase of 4%; of which near-line HDD shipments are expected to reach 75.6 million units, a year-on-year increase of 12%, accounting for the main part of the overall HDD demand. The production end also remains high.

Total HDD production in 2026 is expected to be 134.9 million units, of which near-line HDD production is 84.37 million units, higher than 74.77 million units in 2025.

In terms of price, the research report pointed out that in 2026, HDD manufacturers will no longer rely solely on expanding near-line HDD shipments or production capacity, but will be more active in promoting single-machine capacity increases, thereby driving up the average unit price, and at the same time starting to increase prices on the basis of the same products.

Discussions on long-term supply agreements (LTA) for 2030 have already occurred in the industry, and Goldman Sachs expects the price increase trend to continue. Data shows that the overall HDD ASP is expected to rise from US$180.1 in 2025 to US$236.0 in 2026; industry revenue is expected to increase from US$22.291 billion to US$30.478 billion.

In terms of parts and components, Goldman Sachs said that the current material and component bottlenecks have eased than before, but some materials may still become constraints again in the future. Technological upgrades may drive up ASPs, but there is no clear trend yet in direct price increases for the same product.

Beneficiaries mainly include the increase in the number of components, the increase in unit price brought about by technology upgrades, and the increase in the near-line HDD market share of suppliers. Among them, TDK benefits from the increased demand for HDD head outsourcing.

According to the research report, starting from the April to June quarter, the proportion of Western Digital and Seagate outsourcing magnetic heads to TDK is expected to increase significantly.

TDK has previously disclosed that HDD magnetic head shipments are expected to increase by 40% in FY3/27, of which near-line HDD magnetic head shipments are expected to increase by 50%. Goldman Sachs gave TDK a 12-month target price of 4,600 yen and HOYA a target price of 33,000 yen.

Bank of America: Nvidia’s valuation has reflected excessively pessimistic expectations and maintains a buy rating In its latest research report, Bank of America maintained a "buy" rating for Nvidia with a target price of $350, believing that the market's concerns about its AI growth prospects have been amplified.

The research report pointed out that although Nvidia maintains its dominant position in the field of AI chips, its stock price rose only 3% during the year, significantly lagging behind the 82% increase of the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index.

The current forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18 times is at a seven-year low, and the valuation already implies a discount of approximately 30% to 35% to earnings per share from 2027 to 2028.

Bank of America believes that investors are mainly worried about four points: rising storage costs such as HBM compressing gross profit margins, competition from self-developed ASICs by cloud vendors, crowded institutional holdings, and NVIDIA's investment in suppliers and customers that may affect cash returns.

However, the bank judged that the market underestimated Nvidia’s pricing power, supply chain scale and full-stack ecological advantages.

On the cost side, Bank of America predicts that the cost of a single rack HBM will only increase by about US$200,000 to US$300,000 from Blackwell to Rubin, while the selling price of the entire rack may rise from US$3 million to US$4 million to US$6 million to US$7 million, which is enough to offset the storage cost pressure, and the gross profit margin is expected to remain in the mid-range of about 75%.

The company's supply chain advance commitments of more than $119 billion also help secure storage, wafer, packaging and power supplies. In terms of competition, Bank of America pointed out that Nvidia's GPU accelerator revenue has increased approximately 700 times since the launch of Google TPU in 2015.

Nvidia's recent sales to hyperscale cloud vendors have increased by 115% year-on-year, nearly twice the growth rate of cloud capital expenditures, showing that its share of cloud vendor wallets is still increasing. Bank of America predicts that Nvidia can maintain a share of more than 65% to 70% of AI capital expenditures in the long term.

Bank of America also predicts that the AI data center system market will increase from US$273 billion in 2025 to approximately US$1.7 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 44%; of which the AI accelerator market will reach approximately US$1.1 trillion.

Based on this assumption, Nvidia's earnings per share are expected to rise from more than $13 in 2027 to more than $25 in 2030. Risks include AI project fluctuations, gaming business cycles, export restrictions to China, slowing capital returns, and regulatory scrutiny.

Goldman Sachs raises Amazon target price to $335, saying AI investment cycle is still a mid- to long-term buying point Goldman Sachs maintained a "buy" rating in Amazon's second-quarter earnings forecast report and raised its 12-month target price to $335 from $325.

The bank believes that although Amazon’s stock price fluctuated and weakened this quarter, the core controversies surrounding consumers, AI capital expenditures, AWS growth, Trainium’s self-developed chips and smart shopping have not changed the logic of medium- and long-term revenue compound growth and profit margin expansion.

On the consumer side, Goldman Sachs believes that Amazon users as a whole are still stable. Recent Prime Day sales have increased by 9.5% year-on-year. However, the structure shows trends such as greater price sensitivity, consumption tilting towards necessities, and purchasing power being more concentrated in high-income households.

AWS is still the focus of research reports. Goldman Sachs raised its AWS revenue and capital expenditure forecasts due to factors including AI computing power demand continuing to exceed supply, backlog conversion, traditional workload migration, production capacity online, and some pricing adjustments starting from July 1.

The bank expects AWS revenue to grow by approximately 33% year-on-year in 2026, approximately 35% in 2027, and a four-year compound growth rate of approximately 29% from 2025 to 2029. At the same time, AI infrastructure construction will significantly drive up capital expenditures.

Goldman Sachs estimates that Amazon's cumulative capital expenditures from 2026 to 2028 will be approximately US$827 billion; the cost of each additional 1GW of data center capacity will be approximately US$50 billion to US$60 billion, of which US$15 billion will be infrastructure such as land and electricity, and US$35 billion to US$45 billion will be IT hardware such as servers and storage.

Amazon's data center capacity in 2025 will be about 6.85GW, and it plans to double it by 2027. Goldman Sachs estimates that AWS capital expenditures in 2027 may reach US$200 billion to US$250 billion.

In terms of capital requirements, Goldman Sachs predicts that high investment in AWS will cause Amazon's total free cash flow to have a gap of approximately US$70 billion from 2027 to 2028, of which FCFE may turn negative to US$41 billion in 2027.

However, the bank believes that Amazon's negative working capital model, higher cash reserves, low leverage and bond market financing capabilities are sufficient to cover the medium-term funding gap.

Goldman Sachs also said that Trainium 2 is "basically sold out" and Trainium 3 is "nearly fully booked." The annual revenue of self-developed chips is about 20 billion US dollars, and if sold to third parties, it can reach 50 billion US dollars.

The bank believes that Trainium will not replace Nvidia GPUs, but it is expected to reduce the cost of AI inference and become a driving force for AWS's long-term profit margin improvement. (

AI-driven businesses represented 74% of net sales, up 104% YoY, triggering analyst upgrades (Stifel $75, Citizens $85, Needham $80). $NVDA $DELL $MU

6. $BABA $AVGO among the bright spots in a red session. 

Winners: Alibaba +11.05%, Akamai +10.67%, Arista +8.76%, Broadcom +4.83%. Losers: Palo Alto Networks -4.88%, DoorDash -3.25%, Palantir -1.6%. $PLTR $PANW $ANET

7. IMF downgrades global outlook; sees oil up ~32% in 2026. 

The IMF expects oil to rise nearly 32% and global consumer prices to climb 4.7% (up from 4.1% in 2025), signaling stalled inflation progress. Its forecast assumes the Strait of Hormuz reopens later this month, normalizing by March 2027.

8. Markets close mixed — Dow worst, Nasdaq holds. 

The Dow dropped 576.76 points (-1.09%) to 52,348.39, the S&P fell 0.28% to 7,482.71, and the Nasdaq bucked the trend to rise 0.2% to 25,870.65 — buoyed by a chip rebound (Broadcom, Nvidia) even as oil-driven inflation fears pressured the broader tape.

Crude oil prices surged more than 7% Wednesday after President Trump announced that the ceasefire with Iran was over, following attacks on commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz. Several oil and gas tankers turned back after the announcement.

Additionally, President Trump warned of continued strikes on Iranian resources, leaving open the possibility of taking over Kharg Island, Iran's primary oil export hub.

On Wednesday, Iran also threatened to strike "enemy" targets at a ratio of at least 2:1 in response to any future attack on its territory or interests.

The International Monetary Fund cut its 2026 growth forecast for the Middle East to 0.7%, down 1.2 percentage points from April. However, in its World Economic Outlook published Tuesday, the fund said it expects growth to rebound to 6.5% in 2027, up 1.9 percentage points from the previous estimate.

According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, U.S. petroleum exports reached a record high in April as disruptions to crude oil and refined product flows through the Strait of Hormuz increased global demand for U.S. exports. Crude oil accounted for most petroleum exports, averaging 5.6 million barrels per day in April, 21% above the previous record set in December 2023.

Crude oil refinery inputs were 173,000 barrels per day below the previous week's average but still totaled 17.0 million barrels per day. Refineries operated at 95.8% of operable capacity last week.

The global crude benchmark, Brent, also surged Wednesday, with August contracts up more than 6.5%. Heating oil also saw large moves. Front-month /HO contracts rose more than 13%, reaching a high of 3.7714, a level not seen since early June.

Technicals

Front-month crude traded around 73.62 on Wednesday afternoon, briefly moving above its 20- and 200-day simple moving averages. However, prices remain below the levels reached at the start of the Iran conflict in late February, when crude quickly climbed to 119.48 and entered a period of sharp volatility.

The RSI stood at 41.1554 after crossing back above the 30-line on Monday. The RSI fell below the 30-line on June 22 before briefly crossing back above it. Asian Stocks Diverge as South Korean Chipmakers Lead the Rebound

📈 Asian markets ended mixed on July 9, 2026. South Korea’s Kospi rose 0.6% after falling 5.35% in the previous session, while the Shanghai Composite gained 1.7% and Hong Kong’s Hang Seng declined 0.7%.

💾 South Korean semiconductor stocks were the main bright spot, with SK Hynix rising 5.3%. Renewed buying indicated that investors remain focused on companies directly benefiting from AI and data-center demand.

🌐 Sentiment was also supported by gains in U.S. technology shares, as the Nasdaq advanced 1.3% and Micron climbed 4.5%. Brent crude fell 2.2% to around $76.30 per barrel, easing some short-term macroeconomic pressure.

📊 The session showed that Asia’s recovery remains uneven, but chipmakers continue to lead. The near-term outlook will depend on U.S. technology stocks, oil prices and geopolitical risks.

US🇺🇸 stocks finished higher on Thursday, July 9, 2026, as a surge in technology and chip shares offset renewed geopolitical worries from escalating US-Iran tensions. The Nasdaq Composite jumped 1.27% to 26,198.76, the S&P 500 gained 0.79% to 7,541.83, and the Dow rose 0.25% to 52,481.70, with the PHLX chip index leading for a second straight session. Micron Technology powered much of the advance after outlining plans to invest more than 250 billion dollars in the US through 2035 to meet AI memory demand, while Applied Materials, Sandisk, and other semiconductor names also rallied sharply. Meta Platforms added 4.7% following reports it will manufacture AI chips starting in September. Traders largely downplayed the military exchanges, viewing both sides as incentivized to keep the Strait of Hormuz open and to avoid a full disruption of oil shipments, which helped keep Treasury yields and inflation expectations in check. 

Oil prices seesawed but ultimately pulled back, with Brent crude down 0.62% to 77.54 dollars and US crude off 0.92% to 72.84 dollars a barrel after Iranian forces responded to a second night of US strikes with attacks on military infrastructure in Qatar, Kuwait, and Bahrain. Analysts said the path toward a lasting peace deal remains bumpy, but markets took comfort from signs that both Washington and Tehran were looking to avoid a broader war that would close key shipping lanes. The tech strength was broad, with the iShares Semiconductor ETF surging 3.5% and the Roundhill Memory ETF up 3.7%, while the S&P 500 Information Technology sector gained 1.7% to lead all 11 sectors. UBS Global Wealth Management noted periodic flare-ups could trigger volatility, yet the AI boom and strong corporate earnings expectations continued to underpin risk appetite, with S&P 500 earnings projected to rise 24% year over year led by technology companies. 

- World Business News.
US CFTC suspends CME WTI crude oil futures 24/7 listing. The regulator intervened after CME self-certified the contract despite an ongoing public consultation and unresolved compliance risks.

BREAKING: Brent crude oil has officially broken back above $80/barrel following cancellation of the MOU and Islamabad agreement from both Iran and the US.

Iran has announced automatic full closure of the Strait of Hormuz upon any fresh US strike, which Trump has promised for tonight.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 10, 2026

Market Brief

The State Council issued the "15th Five-Year Plan Carbon Peak Action Plan"

China's June CPI rose 1% year-over-year, PPI increased 4.1%, with both declining month-over-month
China's June automobile sales reached 2.81 million units, with exports and new energy vehicles serving as the primary drivers

A-shares staged a "V-shaped" rebound on Thursday; the Sci-Tech Innovation 50 Index surged over 8%
U.S.-Iran tensions escalate as Iran reportedly rushed over 11 million barrels of crude oil out of the country

Sources say Pakistan is still mediating, potentially reopening the window for U.S.-Iran negotiations
The Federal Reserve released the leadership roster of its "Five Reform Task Forces"

Meta denies excess computing power: renting out capacity can sometimes be more valuable than internal use

Micron plans to expand its U.S. investment to over $250 billion by 2035

OpenAI officially launched the GPT-5.6 series of models

Top Stories

The State Council issued the "15th Five-Year Plan Action Plan for Carbon Peak," aiming for a 17% reduction in carbon dioxide emissions per unit of GDP by 2030 compared to 2025 levels, with non-fossil fuel consumption reaching 25% and new energy vehicle ownership share targeting 30%. The plan outlines five key tasks, including accelerating energy structure optimization, and deploys major projects such as inter-provincial power exchange and clean coal substitution.

The United States has continued military strikes against Iran, targeting approximately 90 Iranian military sites. Iran retaliated with attacks on U.S. military bases across multiple Middle Eastern countries. The White House is preparing for potential sustained combat lasting days or weeks. A U.S. official indicated the escalation could persist for one week to a month, depending on whether Iran continues targeting commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz. Israeli Defense Minister Katz stated Israel’s military is ready to resume operations against Iran. Amid escalating tensions, Iran has reportedly dispatched multiple tankers in 24 hours to export approximately 11 million barrels of crude oil.

Pakistani government sources said Pakistan and Qatar have established new communication channels with the United States and Iran to halt military action and facilitate a return to negotiations under the "Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding." Despite the escalation, Pakistan remains optimistic about preserving the memorandum.

Federal Reserve Chair Warsh has unveiled the leadership roster for five newly formed policy reform task forces, spanning venture capital, technology, academia, and global central banking circles, including prominent figures such as Marc Andreessen, Mervyn King, Gregory Mankiw, and Thomas Sargent. This move is seen as a pivotal step in reshaping the Fed’s policy framework, with focus areas including communication strategy, balance sheet management, economic data metrics, AI’s productivity impact, and inflation targeting.

Meta Platforms CEO Mark Zuckerberg denied claims of excess computing capacity, stating the company is fully utilizing its current resources. He noted that in certain cases, leasing computing power may be more rational than internal use. Internal Meta memos show the company plans to begin mass production of its custom AI chip, “Iris,” in September. By 2027, Meta aims to increase its total computing capacity to 14 gigawatts—roughly double its current deployment.

China Macro & Policy

The General Office of the Communist Party of China Central Committee and the General Office of the State Council issued a notice on comprehensively strengthening flood and drought prevention and emergency response, emphasizing “people first, life first,” early warning, and prevention. It calls for dynamic risk assessments of critical infrastructure.

The National Bureau of Statistics reported that in June, China’s CPI rose 1% year-over-year but fell 0.3% month-over-month due to seasonal factors and international price fluctuations. Core CPI also rose 1% year-over-year. PPI rose 4.1% year-over-year but declined 0.3% month-over-month, as domestic demand improved while international crude oil prices fell.

Nine ministries jointly released the "Opinions on Accelerating Retail Industry Innovation," outlining 20 measures across six directions including spatial planning, supply innovation, and consumption demand creation. The goal is to establish a modern retail system by 2030 that is well-distributed, high-quality, diverse, smart, and competitively orderly.

The "15th Five-Year Plan for Human Resources and Social Security Development" proposes maintaining substantial urban job creation, targeting 25 million re-employment of unemployed urban residents and keeping the urban surveyed unemployment rate below 5.5%.

China Equities Markets

On Thursday, A-shares staged a “V-shaped” rebound. The SSE Index rose 1.65% to 4,036.59, the Shenzhen Component Index gained 3.07%, and the ChiNext Index surged 4.49%. The Wind All China Index climbed 2.42%, with market turnover rising to 2.93 trillion yuan. The semiconductor industry chain exploded, with the Star 50 Index jumping 8.41%—its largest single-day gain in 10 months. Core companies including SMIC, Muxi Semiconductor, and Huahong Grace all hit record highs. The lithium battery chain remained weak, while coal and rare earth sectors declined.

Hong Kong markets rose then retreated. The Hang Seng Index closed down 0.7% at 24,030.18. The Hang Seng Tech Index ended flat after earlier rising over 2%. The Hang Seng China Enterprises Index fell 1.08%. Automotive, consumer goods, and non-ferrous metals sectors declined. Semiconductor stocks rose strongly. AI application stocks diverged: Zhipu AI rose over 11%, while MINIMAX plunged nearly 18%. Southbound funds net bought over 9.8 billion Hong Kong dollars, with Zhipu AI receiving nearly 6.6 billion Hong Kong dollars in net inflows.

Changxin Technology, China’s leading DRAM producer, officially launched its IPO process and will open subscription on July 16. The company plans to issue 6.688 billion shares initially, targeting 29.5 billion yuan in proceeds—the largest IPO in China this year.

The China Securities Regulatory Commission approved Enflame Technology’s registration for its STAR Market IPO, meaning all four major Chinese GPU startups will soon be listed. Enflame Technology aims to raise approximately 6 billion yuan.

China Industry News

The National Health Commission and other agencies officially released the "National Essential Medicines List (2026 Edition)," listing 794 drugs effective from September 1. It added 68 new chemical and biological drugs, 48 traditional Chinese medicine formulations, and 31 pediatric-appropriate formulations. Novo Nordisk’s GLP-1 receptor agonist semaglutide injection for type 2 diabetes was included.

China’s Automobile Association reported June vehicle sales at 2.81 million units, down 3.2% year-over-year. New energy vehicle sales reached 1.643 million units, up 23.6%. Vehicle exports hit 1.037 million units, up 75.1%, with NEV exports at 523,000 units—a 160% year-over-year increase.

Huawei, in collaboration with JD Cloud, Baidu, and over 20 other industry partners, launched the OPEN NPO initiative and initiated China’s first NPO optical interconnect MSA to promote an open, unified near-packaging optical standard system and accelerate next-generation high-speed optical interconnect innovation.

The State-owned Assets Supervision and Administration Commission launched the “Scene Galaxy” initiative in Beijing, unveiling the first batch of ten flagship scenarios by central enterprises, covering AI, coordinated power-carbon systems, green electricity production and storage, and mega-hydroelectric projects.

According to the National Data Bureau, China will leverage 10 national data elements comprehensive pilot zones to first resolve data security and compliance challenges in healthcare, exploring mechanisms for data property rights, circulation, revenue distribution, and governance to integrate digital China with Healthy China.

Fintech

The Bank of Korea submitted a new opinion to the National Assembly’s Finance Committee, reiterating that Korean won stablecoins should be issued primarily by a banking consortium and recommended establishing a policy coordination mechanism involving regulators. The central bank plans to continue its deposit token pilot program in the second half of the year.

The European Union is considering revising its Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) to cover emerging areas including tokenization and non-EU stablecoin issuers. The European Commission is currently seeking feedback from stakeholders.

Corporate News & Updates

Samsung Electronics has raised pricing for new wafer foundry customers by approximately 15%, targeting high-demand advanced nodes such as 4nm and 5nm, and select automotive 8nm nodes. Previously, TSMC has notified major clients of a 5% to 10% price increase.

Micron Technology announced plans to increase its total U.S. investment to over 250 billion dollars by 2035, up 50 billion from its previous 200 billion dollar commitment. Micron aims to raise the share of its DRAM production in the U.S. to 40%.

OpenAI officially launched the GPT-5.6 series, comprising three models: flagship Sol, balanced Terra, and cost-efficient Luna. All are now available via ChatGPT, Codex, and OpenAI API. OpenAI also introduced ChatGPT Work, an AI agent powered by GPT-5.6 capable of autonomously executing tasks across applications and files, tracking projects for hours and converting goals into completed outcomes.

Multiple Tesla supply chain sources said Tesla has finally issued detailed Optimus parts procurement guidelines, requiring suppliers to reach 1,000 units per week by September and scale to 2,000–2,500 units per week by year-end.

Meta launched its latest flagship model, Muse Spark 1.1, which outperformed Google’s Gemini model across agent capability, programming, and multimodal benchmarks. Meta also introduced a paid developer version priced at roughly one-quarter of competitors’ rates.

Jieyue Xingcheng is preparing to launch an AI terminal brand, an agent operating system, and its first AI agent smartphone. The device will integrate multimodal large model capabilities to autonomously perform tasks such as ordering food and booking medical appointments.

Volkswagen Group is drafting a new cost-cutting plan that may involve further layoffs and restructuring of German plants, potentially eliminating up to 100,000–120,000 jobs globally. Plants in Zwickau, Hanover, Emden, and Neckarsulm face potential closure risks.

Global Macro & Policy

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy stated that over the past two weeks, it has gradually reopened the Strait of Hormuz, restoring vessel passage capacity to about 50% of pre-conflict levels. Ships must comply with Iranian safety rules, follow designated routes, and obtain permits from the navy.

New York Fed President Williams said that despite renewed Middle East tensions, energy prices are not expected to remain elevated for the rest of the year. He emphasized that U.S. inflation remains “far above target,” and the Fed will continue basing policy on data and remains firmly committed to bringing inflation down to 2%.

U.S. initial jobless claims fell by 2,000 to 215,000, slightly below the expected 218,000 and near historic lows. However, continuing claims rose to 1.814 million—the highest since March.

The European Central Bank’s June policy meeting minutes showed that due to persistent uncertainty, it is critical to avoid signaling future rate moves. Communication should remain “neutral”—neither implying June’s hike began a new cycle nor suggesting it was a one-off.

Bank of Korea Governor Shin Hyun-soong said that given persistently high inflation, improving growth, and rising financial stability risks, the central bank must raise the policy rate at an appropriate time. The statement reinforced market expectations of a rate hike at next week’s meeting.

The Bank of Japan maintained its assessment of Japan’s nine regions unchanged. Several regions noted export risks from sharp declines and reduced output. Firms continue passing on costs via price hikes, with Middle East conflict-driven raw material cost increases transmitting faster than before.

Germany’s Federal Statistical Office reported that May exports rose 0.9% month-over-month, far exceeding the expected 0.4% decline, marking the fourth consecutive monthly increase. Imports fell 2.5%. Exports to the U.S. surged 23.1% month-over-month.

Global Equity Markets

Most institutions believe oil prices will struggle to sustain gains unless tensions intensify.

The U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission plans to block the Chicago Mercantile Exchange’s application to rapidly launch a 24x7 oil futures contract, citing concerns that energy markets are not yet prepared for a flood of round-the-clock derivatives.

FX & Currency Markets

On Thursday, the onshore CNY/USD rate closed at 6.7935, up 58 basis points from the previous close. The offshore rate closed at 6.7910. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8036, up 41 basis points.

At New York close, the U.S. Dollar Index fell 0.13% to 100.94. Bank of Korea Governor Shin Hyun-soong said that given Korea’s strong economic fundamentals, the won’s appreciation against the dollar is likely. Presidential Policy Chamber Chief Kim Yong-hwan revealed that Korea is discussing international cooperation on exchange rates and is in communication with Samsung Electronics and SK Hynxis.

🔴 BREAKING — This directly extends this week’s Iran-ceasefire-collapse story with a crucial nuance: the crude oil crisis is stabilizing, but the products market — gasoline and diesel specifically — is genuinely tight and getting worse. US crude oil prices are up 5% to $73.90 since Trump’s renewed strikes, but gasoline futures jumped 6.1% and diesel over 10%, far outpacing crude’s move. The national average gas price hit $3.85 Thursday, up from $2.98 when the conflict began.

The core structural problem: US commercial crude inventories just posted their first weekly increase after 10 straight weeks of drawdowns, but the Strategic Petroleum Reserve sits at its lowest level since 1983, and Cushing, Oklahoma’s storage hub has hit operational limits that make further withdrawals difficult. Lipow Oil’s Andy Lipow’s warning is stark: “once the shelf is bare, there’s nowhere to turn” — meaning the only mechanism left to rebalance a real shortage would be demand destruction via higher prices. Kpler’s Matt Smith pinpoints the real mechanism at work: “we’ve lost, over the past four months, 10 million barrels a day out of the Middle East, and yet we’re at $70 a barrel” — offset almost entirely by refineries worldwide running fewer hours, not by genuine spare crude supply. Compounding this: Ukraine’s drone strikes have knocked out meaningful Russian refining capacity, Russia just banned diesel exports to protect domestic supply, and China is only now moving to lift its own product export ban.

💰 YOUR MOVE: This is the more important distinction than the crude-price panic occupying most headlines — refiners and fuel-distribution names may see real margin support from tight product markets even if crude itself stays range-bound, since gasoline and diesel cracks are widening faster than crude prices. Watch Gulf Coast gasoline inventories (7.2 million barrels below normal per OPIS) as the tightest, most immediate pressure point; if China’s product-export lift and rising US refinery runs don’t relieve this fast, expect continued outsized moves in retail fuel prices regardless of what crude itself does. This also complicates the “oil heading to $60” consensus from just last week — that thesis was built on a crude glut, but says nothing about the separate, now-binding constraint in refined products.

Hormuz is semi-closed. 90 targets struck in Iran. The IRGC is charging tolls to cross.

And crude fell 2% today.

How? Five reasons — and a lesson about how oil actually prices. 

1/ The data nobody on your timeline mentions: US crude inventories BUILT 3M barrels — first build since April.

Hormuz friction is 12,000 km away. The barrels in Cushing are here and now. Physical surplus beats distant scarcity, today.

2/ The supply cushion is massive: record Russian seaborne exports, US/Canada/Brazil growing, UAE pumping near-record post-OPEC exit.

Hormuz at 75% capacity removes ~1.7-2 mb/d. The cushion absorbs most of it. Without a FULL closure, the shock is manageable — and the market knows it.

3/ The subtle one: markets price BARRELS, not sovereignty.

Read the IRGC statement like a physical trader: Iran says traffic is recovering to 50% under ITS permit system. Iran wants the strait OPEN — it monetizes the toll, not the closure.

And the US is striking anti-ship capability while leaving Iran's energy infrastructure untouched.

Both sides are signaling: the barrels keep flowing

4/ Crude wasn't pricing "war yes/no." It was pricing a distribution where full closure had real weight.

Every day of surgical strikes + no energy targets + tolls instead of blockade NARROWS that tail. The premium attached to it gets refunded — even while visible friction (14 transits, dark shipping) gets worse.

Physical friction and price can diverge. They measure different things.

5/ Plus the macro headwind: the same energy shock feeds inflation → hawkish Fed → stronger dollar → lower crude.

Macro loops are never one-way.

6/ The lesson:

Markets trade the second derivative. Not "is there war?" but "is there MORE war than yesterday's price assumed?"

Today's answer was no. So the tape sold off while the strait stayed semi-closed.

Headlines measure drama. Price measures surprise.

Liberia🇱🇷 has become one of the world’s biggest shipping powers despite its small size, holding the title of the largest ship registry for three consecutive years. According to Lloyd’s List, Liberia’s registered ship tonnage approached 300 million gross tons as of November 1, 2025, a 3.2 percent increase over 2024 and nearly 45 million gross tons ahead of its nearest rival. In June 2025, the Liberian International Ship and Corporate Registry became the first ever to surpass 300 million gross tons, reaching 307.3 million gross tons with 6,092 vessels under its flag, representing 17 percent of the global fleet. Liberia overtook Panama in 2023 to claim the top spot, a position Panama had held since 1993, and now controls 16.39 percent of the world’s market share. It also operates the largest container ship fleet at 23.37 percent of global container tonnage and flags over 15.25 percent of the world’s oil tanker fleet.

The registry’s rise is built on an open registry model launched in 1949 with the vessel World Peace, offering competitive fees, streamlined remote registration, and strong regulatory compliance. Liberia maintains top tier status on the White Lists of both the Paris and Tokyo Memoranda of Understanding on Port State Control, reflecting strong safety performance from 2022 to 2024. In June 2025, United Against a Nuclear Iran commended the registry for a world class compliance programme after finding Liberian flagged vessels were not involved in crude oil smuggling networks. The Liberia Maritime Authority says the registry generates major national revenue through registration fees, inspections, taxes, and certification services. Senior officer Alfonso Castillero attributes growth to worldwide services and innovation, including remote vessel registrations during the COVID pandemic and partnerships to develop hydrogen powered cargo ships. The combination of scale, compliance, and responsiveness has made Liberia the trusted flag of choice for shipowners worldwide.
